description: Metrics, Economic Buyer, Decision Criteria, Decision Process, Paper Process, Identified Pain, Champion, Competition - plus Security Blockers, Technical Validation, and Expansion Hypothesis

sections.title: Metrics
sections.subtitle: Quantifiable business outcomes the economic buyer cares about
prompts: What specific metrics will the economic buyer use to measure success?
What is the current baseline for each metric?
What improvement targets have been agreed upon?
How will ROI be calculated and reported to leadership?

sections.title: Economic Buyer
sections.subtitle: Person with budget authority and final sign-off
prompts: Who controls the budget for developer tooling?
What are their top 3 priorities this quarter?
What other initiatives compete for the same budget?
How do they prefer to receive ROI justification?

sections.title: Decision Criteria
sections.subtitle: Requirements and weighted evaluation criteria
prompts: What are the must-have vs. nice-to-have requirements?
How is each criterion weighted in the vendor scorecard?
What technical validation is required before purchase?
What security/compliance gates must be passed?

sections.title: Decision Process
sections.subtitle: Stages, stakeholders, and timeline to purchase
prompts: What are the stages from evaluation to purchase?
Who is involved and signs off at each stage?
What is the expected timeline?
What could accelerate or delay the process?

sections.title: Paper Process
sections.subtitle: Procurement, legal, and contracting requirements
prompts: What procurement portal and process must be followed?
Are there standard contract templates or red-line requirements?
What insurance/indemnification is required?
Who are the legal and procurement contacts?

sections.title: Identified Pain
sections.subtitle: Business problems driving the initiative
prompts: What is the core business pain being addressed?
What is the quantified cost of inaction?
Who feels this pain most acutely?
Has a previous solution failed? Why?

sections.title: Champion
sections.subtitle: Internal advocate driving the deal forward
prompts: Who is your primary champion and their title?
What is their influence level and personal motivation?
How can you enable them to sell internally?
Do they have a track record of successful tool adoption?

sections.title: Competition
sections.subtitle: Alternatives, status quo, and differentiation
prompts: What alternatives is the buyer evaluating?
What is the status quo and its hidden costs?
What is our differentiation for this specific account?
What objections do competitors raise about Cursor?

sections.title: Security Blockers
sections.subtitle: CISO concerns, compliance gates, and risk mitigation
prompts: What security concerns has the CISO or InfoSec team raised?
Which compliance frameworks apply (SOC 2, PCI, HIPAA, GDPR)?
Are there data residency or restricted-environment requirements?
What evidence or documentation is needed to unblock?

sections.title: Technical Validation Plan
sections.subtitle: POC scope, success criteria, and engineering evaluation
prompts: What is the POC/pilot scope (teams, repos, duration)?
What are the technical success criteria?
Which engineering teams are involved in validation?
What integrations must be tested (SSO, GitHub, CI/CD)?

sections.title: Expansion Hypothesis
sections.subtitle: Land-and-expand strategy beyond initial deployment
prompts: What is the initial land (seats, teams, use case)?
What is the 12-month expansion path?
What triggers expansion to additional teams or business units?
What is the total addressable seat count and ACV potential?

mockAccounts.name: Meridian National Bank
mockAccounts.aeNotes: Tier-1 strategic account. Heavy regulatory burden but strong champion in Platform Engineering. Copilot already deployed to 300 users - position Cursor as IDE replacement.

content.metrics:
Primary metrics:
- PR cycle time: baseline 5.1 days -> target 3.5 days (31% reduction)
- Developer onboarding ramp: 12 weeks -> 8 weeks
- Security review cycle for code changes: 3.2 days -> 2.0 days
- Annual productivity value: $14.2M across 1,200 developers

Measurement: GitHub Enterprise analytics, DORA dashboard, quarterly VP Eng review.
ROI threshold: 3x return within 12 months to justify enterprise-wide rollout.
content.economic-buyer:
Economic Buyer: David Okonkwo, EVP & CTO
Budget authority: $2.4M annual dev tooling budget

Top priorities:
1. Digital transformation velocity for mobile banking platform
2. Regulatory compliance automation (reduce manual audit prep)
3. Developer retention in competitive NYC talent market

Prefers: 1-page executive brief + live demo. Decision tied to Q4 budget cycle.
Competing spend: GitHub Copilot renewal ($180K), Datadog expansion, contractor reduction initiative.
content.decision-criteria:
Must-haves (deal-breakers):
- SOC 2 Type II + financial services references
- Privacy Mode / zero code retention
- SSO via Okta + SCIM provisioning
- Enterprise security documentation, privacy controls, and vendor risk review

Weighted scorecard:
- Security & compliance (35%)
- Productivity impact (25%)
- Total cost of ownership (20%)
- Integration with GitHub Enterprise (10%)
- Vendor stability & support (10%)

Technical POC required with Platform + Core Banking teams before procurement.
content.decision-process:
Stage 1: Security pre-screen (InfoSec) - IN PROGRESS
Stage 2: 90-day pilot with Platform team (150 devs) - PLANNED Q3
Stage 3: Vendor scorecard review (Procurement + Eng leadership)
Stage 4: Legal/MSA review (6-8 weeks typical)
Stage 5: CTO + CRO joint sign-off

Timeline: 7-9 months from pilot start to enterprise agreement.
Accelerators: Strong pilot metrics, CISO pre-approval on privacy mode.
Risks: Q4 budget freeze, regulatory audit in Q3 diverting eng resources.
content.paper-process:
Procurement: Ariba vendor portal registration required
Contract: Standard MSA + Financial Services DPA addendum
Insurance: $10M cyber liability, $5M E&O minimum
Data Processing Agreement: Required with sub-processor disclosure

Contacts:
- Jane Park, Director Vendor Management
- David Kim, Senior Counsel (Technology)
- Raj Patel, InfoSec Vendor Risk

Typical legal cycle: 8-10 weeks. Precedent: GitHub Enterprise MSA took 11 weeks.
content.identified-pain:
Core pain: Engineering velocity declining 15% YoY while regulatory requirements grow 30%. Mobile banking roadmap delayed 2 quarters.

Cost of inaction:
- $22M in delayed digital feature revenue
- $3.1M/year in contractor backfill for velocity gaps
- Developer attrition at 18% (above industry avg) - exit interviews cite 'outdated tooling'

Pain owners: VP Platform Engineering, Head of Digital Banking, 1,200 developers spending 30%+ time on boilerplate and compliance scaffolding.
content.champion:
Primary: Sarah Chen, VP Platform Engineering
Influence: High - reports to CTO, respected across all eng teams
Motivation: Team morale, velocity OKRs, personal brand as innovation leader

Secondary: Marcus Webb, Principal Engineer (technical validator, GitHub admin)

Enablement delivered: Executive ROI deck, security FAQ for CISO, champion playbook with weekly office hour templates.
Track record: Led GitHub Enterprise adoption (2022) and InnerSource program (2023) - both successful.
content.competition:
Active alternatives:
- GitHub Copilot Business - 300 seats deployed, $180K/yr renewal in Q4
- Status quo (manual coding + internal code search tools)
- Evaluated Codeium Q1 2025 - rejected on security grounds

Differentiation for Meridian:
- Full IDE vs. plugin - codebase context across monorepo
- Agent mode for compliance scaffolding and test generation
- Enterprise admin controls Copilot lacks

Key objection: 'We already have Copilot' -> Position as replacement, not addition. Validate workflow impact vs. Copilot in a head-to-head pilot.
content.security-blockers:
Active blockers:
1. CISO requires clarity on data handling, subprocessors, and retention policies
2. Model training opt-out must be contractually guaranteed (not just policy)
3. PCI-scoped repositories must be excluded from AI indexing - need .cursorignore at scale
4. Pen test results required before production pilot

Mitigations in progress:
- Privacy Mode documentation shared with InfoSec (approved in principle)
- SOC 2 Type II report under NDA review
- Proposed pilot scope excludes PCI/CUI repos entirely

Blocker owner: Raj Patel (InfoSec Vendor Risk) - weekly sync scheduled.
content.technical-validation:
Pilot scope:
- Teams: Platform Engineering (80 devs) + Developer Experience (40 devs)
- Duration: 90 days
- Repos: 12 non-PCI microservices + 2 monorepo modules

Success criteria:
- >75% weekly active usage
- 20%+ PR cycle time reduction (measured via GitHub Enterprise)
- Developer satisfaction >4.2/5
- Zero security policy violations

Integrations to validate:
- Okta SSO + SCIM group mapping
- GitHub Enterprise Cloud (PR analytics, CODEOWNERS)
- Internal linter/formatter configs (ESLint, Prettier, custom rules)

Technical sponsor: Marcus Webb, Principal Engineer
content.expansion-hypothesis:
Land: 120 seats (Platform + DevEx teams) - $57.6K ACV

Expansion path:
- Month 4-6: Core Banking engineering (350 devs) - triggered by pilot metrics
- Month 7-9: Digital Banking + Mobile (400 devs)
- Month 10-12: Wealth Management + Operations IT (330 devs)

Total addressable: 1,200 developers = $576K ACV
Expansion trigger: >20% cycle time improvement + CISO sign-off on expanded repo scope

Upside: Cursor Agent for automated compliance documentation could expand to Legal/Compliance teams (non-dev seats).

mockAccounts.name: ShopNova Retail Group
mockAccounts.aeNotes: Mid-market retailer modernizing e-commerce platform. Bottom-up interest from frontend teams. Need to elevate to VP Engineering for budget. Seasonal hiring spikes create onboarding pain.

content.metrics:
Primary metrics:
- Feature delivery velocity: 8 epics/quarter -> target 12 epics/quarter
- E-commerce checkout page deploy frequency: 1x/week -> 3x/week
- New developer time-to-first-PR: 6 weeks -> 3 weeks
- Holiday season code freeze recovery time: 2 weeks -> 3 days

Baseline captured via Jira + GitHub metrics. Target ROI: $3.8M annual productivity value.
Economic buyer wants simple metric: 'Can we ship the holiday platform rewrite on time?'
content.economic-buyer:
Economic Buyer: Lisa Tran, VP Engineering
Budget authority: ~$800K dev tooling (shared with QA and DevOps tools)

Priorities:
1. Holiday 2026 platform rewrite (hard deadline: September)
2. Reduce contractor dependency ($1.2M/year spend)
3. Improve e-commerce conversion through faster experimentation

Budget cycle: Annual, resets February. Prefers vendor comparison matrix + peer references from retail/e-commerce.
Competing: Datadog renewal, Shopify Plus integration project, headcount freeze.
content.decision-criteria:
Must-haves:
- VS Code compatibility (90% of team on VS Code)
- GitHub Cloud integration
- SOC 2 Type II
- Admin dashboard for usage tracking

Nice-to-haves:
- Slack integration for Agent notifications
- Multi-language support (TypeScript, Python, Java)

Evaluation: Informal scorecard - VP Eng decides with CTO input. No formal RFP expected under $250K.
content.decision-process:
Stage 1: Team-level trial (30 days, 25 devs) - PROPOSED
Stage 2: VP Eng review of trial results
Stage 3: Budget approval from CTO (Lisa's manager)
Stage 4: Procurement via Coupa (simpler process <$250K)
Stage 5: Rollout to e-commerce + platform teams

Timeline: 4-5 months. Can accelerate if holiday deadline pressure increases.
Risk: Engineering bandwidth consumed by platform rewrite - pilot may deprioritize.
content.paper-process:
Procurement: Coupa vendor registration (lightweight for <$250K)
Contract: Standard SaaS agreement acceptable
Insurance: $2M cyber liability (standard threshold)

Contacts:
- Tom Reyes, IT Procurement
- Lisa Tran (also approves contracts under $250K)

Expected legal cycle: 2-3 weeks. Previous SaaS tool purchases (Linear, Vercel) closed in ~3 weeks.
content.identified-pain:
Core pain: E-commerce platform rewrite at risk - 40% of features behind schedule with 5 months to holiday freeze.

Cost of inaction:
- $8M projected revenue loss if holiday features slip
- $1.2M/year in contractor costs to backfill velocity
- Frontend team burnout - 2 senior devs gave notice citing 'death by boilerplate'

Pain acutest in: E-commerce squad (80 devs), Mobile team (35 devs), Platform team (45 devs).
content.champion:
Primary: Alex Rivera, Staff Engineer (E-Commerce)
Influence: Medium - respected IC, no budget authority but trusted by VP Eng
Motivation: Wants to hit holiday deadline, reduce overtime, grow into EM role

Secondary: Priya Sharma, Eng Manager (Mobile) - co-champion, manages 35 devs

Enablement needed: Help Alex build the business case for Lisa Tran. Provide retail peer reference and holiday deadline ROI framing.
Gap: Need to multi-thread to VP Eng level - Alex alone cannot close.
content.competition:
Alternatives:
- GitHub Copilot - not yet deployed, evaluated informally by 10 devs
- Status quo: VS Code + GitHub Copilot free tier (individual accounts, shadow IT concern)
- Replit Ghostwriter - rejected (no enterprise controls)

Differentiation:
- Agent mode for full feature scaffolding (checkout flows, A/B test variants)
- Enterprise admin to eliminate shadow IT Copilot usage
- Codebase-aware context across Shopify Hydrogen + custom React monorepo

Objection: 'Too expensive for our size' -> Frame against $1.2M contractor spend.
content.security-blockers:
Blockers (lower severity than bank):
1. IT wants SSO before any rollout beyond trial - Okta available
2. Customer PII in e-commerce repos - need .cursorignore for customer data schemas
3. PCI-scoped payment modules must be excluded from indexing

No active CISO blockers - InfoSec review is lightweight for this deal size.
Mitigation: Privacy Mode + repo allowlist for pilot. Payment modules excluded.
content.technical-validation:
Trial scope:
- Team: E-Commerce frontend (25 devs)
- Duration: 30 days (aligned with sprint cycle)
- Repos: 3 Hydrogen storefront repos + shared component library

Success criteria:
- 2+ features shipped faster than sprint baseline
- >70% weekly active usage
- Qualitative: team wants to continue after trial

Integrations: GitHub Cloud, Shopify CLI, ESLint/Prettier configs, Vercel deployment pipeline.
Technical sponsor: Alex Rivera, Staff Engineer.
content.expansion-hypothesis:
Land: 80 seats (E-Commerce team) - $38.4K ACV

Expansion:
- Month 2-3: Mobile team (35 devs) + Platform (45 devs)
- Month 4-6: Backend/API teams (120 devs) + DevOps (40 devs)
- Month 7-12: Data Engineering (50 devs) + internal tools (80 devs)

Total addressable: 450 developers = $216K ACV
Trigger: Holiday rewrite on track + VP Eng budget approval for FY2027.

mockAccounts.name: CloudPulse SaaS
mockAccounts.aeNotes: Developer-native company - fast-moving deal. Already completed successful 60-day pilot. CTO is economic buyer and champion. Competing with internal build vs. buy debate for AI coding tooling.

content.metrics:
Metrics (already baselined from 60-day pilot):
- PR cycle time: 3.8 days -> 2.4 days (37% reduction, validated)
- Test coverage on new code: 62% -> 81%
- Time spent on boilerplate/scaffolding: 8 hrs/wk -> 2 hrs/wk per dev
- Developer NPS: 32 -> 58

Annual productivity value: $4.6M (320 devs - $165K loaded - 8.7% effective gain).
CTO wants to publish an internal case study if enterprise deal closes.
content.economic-buyer:
Economic Buyer: James Park, CTO & Co-founder
Budget authority: Direct - no procurement committee for tools under $500K

Priorities:
1. Maintain engineering velocity as headcount growth slows (20% -> 10% hiring)
2. AI-native product positioning - 'we use AI to build AI'
3. Reduce time-to-market for enterprise features (top revenue driver)

Decision style: Fast - pilot-to-purchase in 90 days if metrics hold.
Competing: Headcount budget (3 eng hires vs. Cursor seats), internal AI tooling build.
content.decision-criteria:
Must-haves:
- Agent mode with multi-file editing
- API access for custom integrations
- Privacy Mode (customer code is sensitive)
- Usage analytics API for internal dashboard

Decision criteria (CTO's framework):
- Developer love (NPS/satisfaction) - 40%
- Measured productivity gain - 30%
- Build vs. buy cost analysis - 20%
- Enterprise admin & security - 10%

Pilot already validated all must-haves. Remaining: finalize build vs. buy analysis.
content.decision-process:
COMPLETED: 60-day pilot (180 devs, all metrics exceeded)
CURRENT: Enterprise agreement negotiation
NEXT: CTO sign-off -> Finance approval -> Rollout to remaining 140 devs

Timeline: Close within 30 days.
Accelerators: Pilot success, CTO is co-founder with direct authority.
Risk: Internal platform team proposing to build custom AI coding layer (build vs. buy debate).
content.paper-process:
Procurement: Minimal - CTO + CFO sign SaaS agreement directly
Contract: Standard Cursor enterprise MSA acceptable
Special terms: API access SLA, priority support for production incidents

Contacts:
- James Park, CTO (signatory)
- Nina Okoro, CFO (budget approval)
- Legal: External counsel (light review, ~1 week)

Expected close: 2 weeks for paper.
content.identified-pain:
Pain: Headcount growth slowing but product roadmap accelerating 35%. Need to 'do more with the same team.'

Cost of inaction:
- 2 enterprise features delayed -> $2.1M ARR impact
- Internal build proposal would consume 4 platform engineers for 6+ months ($800K opportunity cost)
- Losing candidates to competitors citing better AI tooling

CTO's personal pain: Board asking 'What's your AI strategy for engineering productivity?'
content.champion:
Primary: James Park, CTO & Co-founder
Influence: Ultimate - IS the economic buyer
Motivation: Board narrative, engineering culture, competitive positioning

Secondary: Elena Vasquez, VP Engineering - operational champion, ran the pilot

Enablement: Build vs. buy analysis comparing internal build ($800K, 6 months) vs. Cursor ($154K/yr). Internal case study template for company blog.
Strongest champion profile - no multi-threading needed.
content.competition:
Primary competitor: Internal build
- Platform team wants to build custom AI coding layer on top of LLM APIs
- Estimated: 4 engineers - 6 months = $800K+ opportunity cost
- CTO leaning buy after pilot results

External:
- GitHub Copilot - used by 40 devs individually, no enterprise contract
- Cursor already won the pilot head-to-head (37% vs. 18% cycle time improvement)

Differentiation: Agent mode depth, codebase indexing, and time-to-value (days vs. months to build).
content.security-blockers:
Minimal blockers - developer-native company with pragmatic security:
1. Privacy Mode confirmed and enabled (required for customer code)
2. SOC 2 report reviewed and accepted
3. API key management for usage analytics integration

No CISO gate - CTO makes security decisions directly.
Status: CLEARED during pilot.
content.technical-validation:
COMPLETED - 60-day pilot results:
- 180 developers across Platform, Product, and Infrastructure
- 37% PR cycle time reduction (exceeded 25% target)
- 81% test coverage on new code (exceeded 75% target)
- 58 developer NPS (exceeded 45 target)

Remaining validation: API integration for internal usage dashboard (2-week sprint).
Technical sponsor: Elena Vasquez, VP Engineering.
content.expansion-hypothesis:
Land: 320 seats (full engineering org) - $153.6K ACV

Expansion opportunities:
- Customer Success engineering team (40 devs building integrations) - Month 3
- Solutions Engineering (15 devs, demo scripting) - Month 6
- Potential: Offer Cursor as part of CloudPulse's 'AI-native' customer story

No traditional land-and-expand needed - this is a full-org deal.
Upside: Reference account for SaaS/developer tools vertical. Case study + conference talk.

mockAccounts.name: Streamline Media Corp
mockAccounts.aeNotes: Large media company with content + engineering orgs. Content engineering and streaming platform teams are entry points. IP protection is paramount. Multiple business units create complex land-and-expand.

content.metrics:
Primary metrics:
- Streaming feature release cadence: 2 releases/month -> 4 releases/month
- Content metadata pipeline processing time: 4 hrs -> 1 hr per batch
- Cross-platform (iOS/Android/Web/TV) feature parity gap: 6 weeks -> 2 weeks
- Legacy CMS migration velocity: 12 modules/quarter -> 20 modules/quarter

Projected annual value: $9.8M across 680 developers.
SVP Digital wants metric tied to subscriber retention: faster A/B test deployment -> conversion lift.
content.economic-buyer:
Economic Buyer: Rachel Morrison, SVP Digital Technology
Budget authority: $4.5M digital engineering budget (shared across 3 BUs)

Priorities:
1. Streaming platform modernization (replace legacy CMS by 2027)
2. AI-driven content personalization (engineering enabler)
3. Reduce agency/contractor spend on content tooling ($4M/year)

Budget cycle: Annual planning in November. Prefers business case with media industry peer references.
Competing: Adobe Experience Cloud renewal, AWS cost optimization, content AI initiative.
content.decision-criteria:
Must-haves:
- IP protection - no training on proprietary content code
- SOC 2 Type II + media industry references
- Support for polyglot stack (Python, Swift, Kotlin, TypeScript, Go)
- SSO via Azure AD

Weighted criteria:
- IP/security protection (30%)
- Multi-platform dev productivity (25%)
- Cost vs. contractor spend (25%)
- Admin controls & compliance (20%)

Unique requirement: Legal review of AI-generated code IP ownership.
content.decision-process:
Stage 1: Qualification & discovery - CURRENT
Stage 2: Content Engineering pilot (50 devs, 60 days)
Stage 3: Streaming Platform pilot (parallel, 80 devs)
Stage 4: BU-level approval (3 SVPs must align)
Stage 5: Enterprise procurement (formal RFP likely at this ACV)
Stage 6: Legal review (IP clauses, AI-generated code ownership)

Timeline: 9-12 months. Media companies move slowly on IP-sensitive tools.
Risk: Legal/IP review could add 3+ months. Content AI initiative may absorb budget.
content.paper-process:
Procurement: SAP Ariba + formal RFP process expected above $300K
Contract: Custom MSA required - standard terms rarely accepted
Special clauses: AI-generated code IP ownership, content code confidentiality, right-to-audit
Insurance: $10M cyber + media liability coverage

Contacts:
- Patricia Wu, VP Procurement
- Alan Foster, General Counsel (IP specialist)
- Diane Huang, VP InfoSec

Expected legal cycle: 12-16 weeks (media IP sensitivity).
content.identified-pain:
Core pain: Legacy CMS migration stalling - 18 months behind schedule, $4M/year in contractor costs to maintain old system.

Cost of inaction:
- $12M in delayed streaming feature revenue
- $4M/year contractor spend on content tooling (vs. $326K Cursor ACV)
- Subscriber churn increasing - competitors shipping personalization features 2x faster

Pain across: Content Engineering (120 devs), Streaming Platform (200 devs), Mobile (160 devs), Data/ML (100 devs).
content.champion:
Primary: Kevin O'Brien, Director of Content Engineering
Influence: Medium - director level, needs SVP sponsorship to proceed
Motivation: CMS migration success, reduce contractor dependency, career advancement

Potential: Amy Liu, VP Streaming Platform - higher influence but not yet engaged

Enablement needed: Media industry reference (similar streaming company), IP protection FAQ for Legal, CMS migration workflow demo.
Gap: Need to elevate from Director to SVP level within 30 days.
content.competition:
Alternatives:
- GitHub Copilot - 150 individual licenses (shadow IT, no enterprise controls)
- Internal 'Content AI' initiative - building custom tooling on GPT-4 APIs
- Agency contractors ($4M/year) - status quo for content tooling
- JetBrains AI Assistant - preferred by 30% of mobile team (Kotlin/Swift)

Differentiation:
- Agent mode for CMS migration (batch convert legacy modules)
- Codebase context across content metadata pipelines
- Enterprise IP protection that individual Copilot licenses lack

Objection: 'Legal won't approve AI on our content code' -> Privacy Mode + IP ownership clause + media peer reference.
content.security-blockers:
Significant blockers:
1. Legal requires contractual guarantee that content code is never used for model training
2. Unreleased content metadata schemas are trade secrets - strict repo isolation needed
3. Azure AD conditional access policies may block Cursor SSO initially
4. Media liability: AI-generated code in production streaming pipeline needs QA review process

Mitigation plan:
- Privacy Mode + custom DPA with IP ownership clause
- Pilot scoped to non-content-critical repos (internal tooling, CMS migration helpers)
- Legal pre-review of AI code ownership terms before pilot starts

Blocker owner: Alan Foster (GC) - meeting scheduled.
content.technical-validation:
Proposed pilot:
- Team: Content Engineering - CMS migration squad (50 devs)
- Duration: 60 days
- Repos: CMS migration tooling (Python/TypeScript), NOT content metadata or streaming core

Success criteria:
- CMS module migration velocity: 12/quarter -> 18/quarter
- >70% weekly active usage
- Legal sign-off on AI-generated code in migration tooling

Phase 2 (parallel): Streaming Platform team (80 devs) - API development and testing.
Integrations: Azure AD SSO, GitHub Enterprise, Jenkins CI, custom Python linters.
Technical sponsor: Kevin O'Brien, Director Content Engineering.
content.expansion-hypothesis:
Land: 50 seats (Content Engineering CMS migration) - $24K ACV

Expansion path:
- Month 3-4: Streaming Platform (200 devs) - $96K
- Month 5-7: Mobile iOS/Android (160 devs) - $76.8K
- Month 8-10: Data/ML Engineering (100 devs) - $48K
- Month 11-12: Ad Tech + Internal Tools (170 devs) - $81.6K

Total addressable: 680 developers = $326.4K ACV
Expansion trigger: CMS migration velocity target met + Legal IP approval for broader scope.

Strategic upside: If Content AI initiative stalls, Cursor becomes the approved AI coding standard across all digital BUs.
